title: Cestrum elegans
taxonomy: Cestrum elegans is classified within the family Solanaceae, commonly known as the nightshade family. This family consists of over 2,600 species, including many economically important plants such as tomatoes, potatoes, and peppers. The genus Cestrum is a group of flowering plants native to the Americas, characterized by their trumpet-shaped flowers and woody stems. Cestrum elegans is a specific species within this genus, known for its delicate white flowers and slender growth habit. By understanding its classification, we can better appreciate the evolutionary relationships between different plant species and their unique characteristics.

side_effects: Cestrum elegans, also known as the Paraguay night-blooming jasmine, is not typically associated with any severe side effects when used as an ornamental plant. However, its sap can cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. Ingesting any part of the plant can be toxic due to the presence of toxic alkaloids, which can lead to symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and stomach pain. intro: Cestrum elegans, also known as the Balsam Apple or Night-blooming Jasmine, is a flowering evergreen shrub native to the tropical regions of Central and South America. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including the treatment of fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions, due to its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. The bioactive constituents of Cestrum elegans include alkaloids, flavonoids, and glycosides, which are responsible for its therapeutic effects. In herbal medicine, Cestrum elegans is commonly prepared as a decoction or tincture, and is used to treat various ailments, including fever, sore throats, and respiratory issues.
